content: LiteBackend opens its own SqlitePool internally via open_sqlite_pool(), so callers who also call open_config_pool() before passing config to LiteBackend::new() end up with two independent pools to the same SQLite file — doubling connections and breaking WAL consistency. Fix: pass the pool into LiteBackend::new() or ensure resolve_runtime() opens exactly one pool and passes it down.

SQLite PRAGMAs set via sqlx::query('PRAGMA ...').execute(&pool) only apply to the single physical connection that happens to execute the query. New connections from the same pool start without the PRAGMA. The correct pattern is SqliteConnectOptions::pragma('busy_timeout', '5000') etc. in the connection options — these are applied to every new connection the pool opens.

SQLite migrations that use ALTER TABLE workarounds (create-new, copy-data, drop-old, rename) MUST be wrapped in a transaction. Without BEGIN/COMMIT, a crash mid-migration leaves the table in an inconsistent state with no clean rollback path. Also: silently dropping rows with unrecognized status values during migration destroys data — unknown statuses should be preserved or the migration should fail fast.

Monolith policy violation — files over 500 lines are a CI hard fail in this repo. workers.rs and runtime.rs both breach the limit. The fix pattern is to split by worker type (one file per worker) or by layer (types, impls, helpers) rather than letting a single file accumulate all cases. Check .monolith-allowlist before adding exceptions.

N+500 anti-pattern — fetching up to 500 rows to answer a boolean question. has_active_jobs() loads a Vec<JobSummary> from all 6 job tables (500 rows each × 6 = up to 3000 rows) just to check .is_empty(). The correct pattern is a single EXISTS query: SELECT 1 FROM table WHERE status IN ('pending','running') LIMIT 1.

LiteServiceRuntime::list_jobs ignores the limit and offset parameters passed by callers. Any pagination built on top of this (web UI, MCP export) silently gets all rows instead of the requested page. Parameters named _limit and _offset (underscore prefix) are a Rust convention for intentionally unused params — in this case the intent was to implement them, not suppress the warning.

ServiceCapabilities flags (export, graph, refresh_schedule, watch_scheduler) exist in ServiceContext but are never checked at the MCP handler layer. Lite-mode clients calling graph or refresh_schedule endpoints get runtime errors or silent failures instead of a clean 'capability not available' response. Capability checks belong at the handler boundary, not just in documentation.

Mixed error bounds across a single trait's methods — some return Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync>, others return Box<dyn Error>. This forces callers to handle two incompatible error types. In async Rust, all trait method errors should be Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync + 'static> (Send + Sync required to send errors across await points and thread boundaries).

FullBackend::cancel_job for GraphJobBackend returns Ok(false) silently when the graph job cancel/cleanup/clear operations fail. Silent Ok returns from cancel operations are dangerous — callers assume the job was stopped and may proceed with dependent work. Errors from cancel/cleanup/clear should propagate as Err, not be swallowed into Ok(false).
